coredata.dc:title: Two swords combination: Smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent and paper sensors for dual-mode detection of glyphosate in edible malt

coredata.dc:description: The long-term and excessive use of glyphosate (GLY) in diverse matrices has caused serious hazard to the human and environment. However, the ultrasensitive detection of GLY still remains challenging. In this study, the smartphone-assisted dual-signal mode ratiometric fluorescent and paper sensors based on the red-emissive gold nanoclusters (R-AuNCs) and blue-emissive carbon dots (B-CDs) were ingeniously designed accurate and sensitive detection of GLY. Upon the presence of GLY, it would quench the fluorescence of B-CDs through dynamic quenching effect, and strengthen the fluorescence response of R-AuNCs due to aggregation-induced enhancement effect. Through calculating the GLY-induced fluorescence intensity ratio of B-CDs to R-AuNCs by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, low to 0.218 μg/mL of GLY could be detected in lab in a wide concentration range of 0.3–12 μg/mL with high recovery of 94.7–103.1% in the spiked malt samples. The smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor achieved in the 96-well plate could monitor 0–11 μg/mL of GLY with satisfactory recovery of 94.1–107.0% in real edible malt matrices for high-throughput analysis. In addition, a portable smartphone-assisted ratiometric paper sensor established through directly depositing the combined B-CDs/R-AuNCs probes on the test strip could realize on-site measurement of 2–8 μg/mL of GLY with good linear relationship. This study provides new insights into developing the dual-signal ratiometric sensing platforms for the in-lab sensitive detection, high-throughput analysis, and on-site portable measurement of more trace contaminants in foods, clinical and environmental samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The long-term and excessive use of glyphosate (GLY) in diverse matrices has caused serious hazard to the human and environment. However, the ultrasensitive detection of GLY still remains challenging. In this study, the smartphone-assisted dual-signal mode ratiometric fluorescent and paper sensors based on the red-emissive gold nanoclusters (R-AuNCs) and blue-emissive carbon dots (B-CDs) were ingeniously designed accurate and sensitive detection of GLY. Upon the presence of GLY, it would quench the fluorescence of B-CDs through dynamic quenching effect, and strengthen the fluorescence response of R-AuNCs due to aggregation-induced enhancement effect. Through calculating the GLY-induced fluorescence intensity ratio of B-CDs to R-AuNCs by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, low to 0.218 μg/mL of GLY could be detected in lab in a wide concentration range of 0.3–12 μg/mL with high recovery of 94.7–103.1% in the spiked malt samples. The smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor achieved in the 96-well plate could monitor 0–11 μg/mL of GLY with satisfactory recovery of 94.1–107.0% in real edible malt matrices for high-throughput analysis. In addition, a portable smartphone-assisted ratiometric paper sensor established through directly depositing the combined B-CDs/R-AuNCs probes on the test strip could realize on-site measurement of 2–8 μg/mL of GLY with good linear relationship. This study provides new insights into developing the dual-signal ratiometric sensing platforms for the in-lab sensitive detection, high-throughput analysis, and on-site portable measurement of more trace contaminants in foods, clinical and environmental samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glyphosate (GLY) has been one of the most commonly used herbicides in the world since the year 2000 due to its fascinating non-selectivity and broad-spectrum activity (Ferrante et al., 2023; Rawat et al., 2023). The global GLY market reached approximately 600,000 to 750,000 tons each year, which will increase to 740,000 to 920,000 tons by 2025 (Maggi et al., 2020). The long-term intensive and excessive use of GLY will inevitably lead to a large amount of residue in the foods, plants, water, soil, and air, posing detrimental effects to human beings and the whole environment due to its poor water solubility, persistent nature and serious genotoxicity, cytotoxicity and neurotoxicity (Costas-Ferreira et al., 2022; González-Moscoso et al., 2023; Rana et al., 2023). Then, it was listed as a possible human carcinogen (Group 2A) by the International Agency for Research on Cancer (IARC) (Bai & Ogbourne, 2016). Although has been interdicted in various countries, GLY still can be monitored in a large quantity of matrices, including the edible and medicinal malt (Chen, 2023; Delprat et al., 2023; Xu et al., 2019). Therefore, it is extremely important and necessary to develop sensitive and reliable analytical methods for GLY monitoring in diverse matrices including edible samples to ensure their quality and safety.

A series of different types of techniques, such as liquid or gas chromatography coupled to various detectors, capillary electrophoresis, and electrochemical strategies, have been proposed for GLY detection in foods, agri-food products, soil, and biological samples (Chen et al., 2023; Gotti et al., 2022; Morimoto et al., 2023; 2021 Wang et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2021; Yu et al., 2022; Zambrano-Intriago et al., 2021), exhibiting high selectivity and thrilling sensitivity. However, these methods either require heavy and expensive instruments, laborious sample pre-treatment and derivatization step, large time-consumption, or are confined to the laboratory bench and low in repeatability, which are incompetent for on-site rapid analysis. More importantly, most of these methods are based on single-signal output modes, which may cause inaccurate results due to the lack of unified standard test control, as well as environmental and sample matrix interferences (Yang et al., 2024; Zhou et al., 2016).

Recently, the dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensing strategy emerges as an innovative a solution to the above limitations (Chi et al., 2023; Li et al., 2024; Yu et al., 2023). Ratio sensing overcomes these challenges and provides higher sensitivity. By integrating the merits of fluorescent sensor and dual-probe signal-based ratiometric mode, this technique has gained excellent priority for trace analysis in diverse fields due to its fascinating advantages: (1) fluorescence signal readout mode exhibits easy operation, fast response, high sensitivity, and outstanding reproducibility (Liu et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021); (2) various nano-labels can be considered as the fluorescent signal probes. For example, carbon dots (CDs) exhibit simple preparation, rich functional groups, good environmental friendliness, and excellent optical characteristics (Zhang et al., 2022), and gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) present large Stokes shift, tunable fluorescence emission, good biocompatibility, high chemical- and photo-stability, and low toxicity (Navarro et al., 2024), which have both attracted increasing interests in constructing diverse fluorescent sensing platforms; (3) the ratio of two fluorescence signals as the output realizes a built-in self-calibration to reduce or even eliminate the external interferences and offset the error from single-emission signal for ensuring more reliable results (Liu et al., 2024); (4) the fluorescence responses of two independent probes with different changing trends can address remarkable signal enhancement to improve the detection sensitivity.

In addition, smartphone-assisted paper sensor has attracted growing attention, especially for trace analytes in resource-constrained circumstance (Lv et al., 2023; Rasheed et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023). Paper sensors provide an environmentally friendly, economical, and user-friendly alternative, which combine the simplicity of paper with the intelligence of scientific research, creating a widely applicable analytical tool (Lv et al., 2018; Malik et al., 2024). As a low-cost and portable substrate, flexible paper with abundant active groups on the surface can be functionalized through different chemical modification techniques to load various fluorescent probes for constructing miniaturized, conveyable, easy-to-operate, and inexpensive sensing platforms (Naksen et al., 2022). Moreover, smartphones have replaced traditional tools to become indispensable in modern life. Thanks to the excellent advancements in camera technology and intelligent software for data analysis, they can rapidly capture the color changes of the recorded fluorescent images from specific reactions with target analytes and quantify the emitted light from excited fluorophores to realize improved sensitivity (Shen et al., 2022; Zeng et al., 2022). All these have promoted smartphone-based paper sensor as a powerful tool for point-of-care (POC) detection of pesticides in a large quantity of samples.

Thus, in this study, an ingenious and universal smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent and paper-based dual-signal mode sensing platform based “Two swords combination” sensing strategy was firstly developed for the in-lab sensitive detection and on-site rapid analysis of GLY in edible malt (Scheme 1 ). First, the non-toxic low-cost blue-emissive CDs (B-CDs) were synthesized with urea and citric acid by using the facile one-step hydrothermal method, and high-yield red-emissive AuNCs (R-AuNCs) were synthesized with bovine serum albumin (BSA)-stabilized chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) (Scheme 2A). Then, B-CDs and R-AuNCs solutions were mixed to form the B-CDs/R-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probes, which displayed uniform morphology and good dispersity with emission peaks at 435 and 635 nm, respectively, under 365 nm single excitation wavelength. Through recording the intensity ratio of the quenched blue signal of B-CDs to the enhanced red fluorescence of R-AuNCs induced by the added GLY using a fluorescence spectrophotometer, as low as 0.218 μg/mL of GLY could be detected within a wide concentration range of 0.3–12 μg/mL. Then, the smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor realized in the 96-well plate that did not rely on the bulky spectrophotometer could monitor 0–11 μg/mL of GLY with good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.996) (Scheme 2B). In addition, a smartphone-assisted paper-based sensor was established through directly depositing the combined B-CDs/R-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probes on the paper strip (Scheme 1B). Under excitation at 365 nm, the color changes of the fluorescent images on the paper caused by the introduced GLY were recorded by a smartphone and automatically transformed into the red, green, and blue color channel values by using the installed ‘Color Grab’ application (App). Then, the ratio value of R to B channel was calculated, which exhibited good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.995) with the concentration of GLY and fascinating reliability for its on-site analysis in edible samples. This study provides new insights into developing the combined dual-signal mode sensing platform for sensitive and portable POC detection of more trace contaminants in a large number of foods, clinical and environmental samples.

Urea and citric acid were obtained from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ethanol and hydrochloric (HCl) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). Carbendazim, acetamiprid, chlorpyrifos, dimethoate, malathion and glyphosate standards were got from Anpel (Shanghai, China). The buffer solutions required in the experiments were obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). All the used chemicals and other reagents are of analytical grade without further purification. Wahaha ultrapure water (Wahaha Foods Co. Ltd., Hangzhou, China) was applied in the experiments.

The structure and particle size of B-CDs and R-AuNCs were characterized by a Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI USA, Inc.). The fluorescence spectrum was obtained by utilizing a FL970 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Techcomp, Shanghai, China). The fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs and R-AuNCs was measured by a JYHORIBA FluoroLog-3 fluorescence spectrometer (Jobin-Yvon, Inc., America), and the zeta potential was studied on a ZS Malvern Zetasizer Nano (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Shanghai, China).

B-CDs with excellent fluorescence properties were synthesized according to the reported research (Qi et al., 2023) with a slight modification. Briefly, 1 g citric acid and 2 g urea was dissolved in 10 mL ultrapure water and sonicated for 3 min. Then, the mixed solution of the precursor molecules was transferred into a 25-mL Teflon-lined reactor and reacted at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature naturally, the B-CDs solution was collected with the addition of 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol for vortexing thoroughly for 10 min, followed by centrifugation at 8000 r/min for another 10 min. The precipitate was collected and re-dissolved in 15 mL of anhydrous ethanol and washed for centrifugation. Finally, the precipitate was dissolved in 15 mL of deionized water and centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min. The newly-obtained precipitate was freeze-dried and stored at low temperature in dark for the following use.

The obtainment of R-AuNCs was referred to the literature with appropriate modifications (Al-Mashriqi et al., 2023). In short, 15 mL of chloroauric acid aqueous solution (10 mM) was mixed thoroughly with 15 mL of BSA solution (50 mg/mL) and stirred intensively in a dark at 37 °C for 20 min, followed by the addition of 1.5 mL of sodium hydroxide solution (1 M) for continuous stirring for 14 h in a dark at 37 °C. The product solution was collected and passed through a 0.22-μm microporous filter membrane (Biosharp, AnHui, China) to remove the large insoluble particles, and was then transferred to a 1000-Da ready-to-use dialysis bag for dialyzing sufficiently in the ultrapure water for 24 h. Finally, the resultant BSA-AuNCs solution was collected and stored at 4 °C prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the B-CDs/R-AuNCs dual-emissive ratiometric fluorescent probes
ce:section.ce:para: The B-CDs/R-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probes were obtained based on a simple mixing of B-CDs and BSA-R-AuNCs solutions. First, the freeze-dried powder of B-CDs was dissolved in ultrapure water to prepare the 0.03 mg/mL aqueous solution. 2 mL of B-CDs solution was mixed with 400 μL of R-AuNCs aqueous solution and vortexed for 1 min to obtain the B-CDs/R-AuNCs dual-signal fluorescent probes, which were stored in a dark at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor for GLY detection
ce:section.ce:para: The B-CDs/R-AuNCs based dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensor was constructed according to the following procedure, as schematically illustrated in Scheme 1A. Briefly, 60 μL of different concentrations of GLY standard solutions was introduced into the 200 μL of B-CDs/R-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe solution, respectively, followed by the addition of 340 μL of ultrapure water to a final volume of 600 μL. Afterwards, the mixed solution was treated by vortexing thoroughly and incubated at room temperature for 6 min. Then, the cuvette containing the mixture was transferred into a fluorescence spectrophotometer to record the fluorescence spectra of B-CDs at 435 nm and R-AuNCs at 635 nm under an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. All experiments were conducted in parallel three times under the same conditions. The calibration equation between the fluorescence (FL) intensity ratio (I 435/I 635) value and the concentration (c) of GLY was plotted for fluorescent quantitation of this pesticide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity evaluation of the proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the proposed dual-signal mode ratiometric fluorescent sensor for target GLY was evaluated with the addition of other five interfering pesticides, including carbendazim, malathion, acetamiprid, dimethoate, and chlorpyrifos. Herein, 200 μL B-CDs/R-AuNCs probe solution was mixed with 60 μL of GLY (8 μg/mL) or the interfering pesticides (16 μg/mL) solution, respectively, with the addition of 340 μL of ultrapure water. After incubation at room temperature for 6 min, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded and the FL intensities of emission peak of R-AuNCs at 635 nm and B-CDs at 435 nm were obtained to calculate the I 435/I 635 value. All experiments were repeated thrice under the same conditions to examine the influences of GLY and five interfering pesticide molecules on the FL emission peak and intensity of the dual-signal probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor for GLY monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: To get rid of the dependence of bulky and expensive fluorescence spectrophotometer, the smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensing system was realized in the 96-well plate for high-throughput analysis of GLY in a large number of samples. Briefly, 180 μL of B-CDs/R-AuNCs probe solution was dripped into each hole of the 96-well plate, followed by the addition of 20 μL of different concentrations (0–11 μg/mL) of GLY solution (Scheme 1B). After shocking and incubation at room temperature for 6 min, each hole containing dual-signal ratiometric probes and GLY was photographed by using a smartphone under a 365-nm ultraviolet (UV) excitation. Then, the RGB data of each image were automatically analyzed by the pre-installed ‘Color Grab’ App (AlternativeTo, Sweden), and the R/B value from the R and B channels was calculated to quantify GLY. To obtain clear fluorescent images, the phone was adjusted to the professional mode with the white balance, shutter time, and ISO to 5500, 1/10, and 40, respectively. In addition, the distance and angle between the mobile phone and the sample solution were controlled rationally to ensure the uniformity of fluorescence images.

To evaluate the analytical reliability and application potential of the above-constructed dual-signal mode ratiometric fluorescent sensing platforms for GLY detection, malt sample with high edible and medicinal values was examined through the recovery tests. 5 g GLY-free (without GLY) malt sample verified by using a LC-MS/MS method was crushed with a high-speed grinder and sieved through a 40-mesh sieve. Then, 0.5 g malt powder was added with 1, 5, and 10 μg/mL of GLY standard solution, and mixed with 5 mL of ultrapure water on a vortex oscillator for 3 min, followed by ultrasonic extraction for 12 min. Afterwards, the mixture was centrifuged at 6000 r/min for 4 min at low temperature, and the supernatant was collected and filtrated through a 0.22-μm microporous membrane to remove the impurities. Finally, the resultant filtrate was collected and analyzed for GLY by using the spectrophotometer-based and smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensors, respectively. And the recovery rate (%) was calculated according to the equation: measured amount/added amount × 100%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted paper-based sensor for GLY measurement
ce:section.ce:para: To improve the on-site intelligent detection ability for GLY in abundant real samples, smartphone-assisted paper-based sensor, as a portable analytical platform, was established by directly depositing the B-CDs/R-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescent probe solution on the flexible paper strip (Scheme 1B). First, the 1 × 1 cm2 paper strip cut from the Whatman No. 1 filter paper was soaked into the ratiometric probe solution for 10 min for the automatic deposition of B-CDs/R-AuNCs nanocomposites, which was then dried in an oven at 40 °C for 40 min to fabricate the ratiometric paper-based sensor. Afterwards, each concentration (0–8 μg/mL) of GLY solution was dripped onto the paper strip and incubated at room temperature for 12 min. Under the UV excitation at 365 nm, the paper strip was photographed with a smartphone with the above-set parameter for obtaining clear fluorescence images. Through using the ‘color grab’ App, the R/B value of dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent probes was obtained to quantify the concentration level of GLY.

Here, the CDs with excellent blue fluorescence emission were simply and rapidly synthesized by using the hydrothermal method with citric acid and urea as the precursor molecules. To further explore the structural characteristics and optical properties of the obtained B-CDs, they were systematically characterized by using a Tecnai G2 F30 S-TWIN TEM, a UV-3660 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan), and a FL970 fluorescence spectrometer.

First, the synthesized B-CDs were examined by TEM and HR (high-resolution)-TEM regarding their structural feature. The TEM images in Fig. 1A indicated that the B-CDs exhibited a homogeneous dispersal state in aqueous solution with clear spherical structures, and the inset image at the top illustrated the high crystallinity of B-CDs with an interplanar distance of 0.21 nm. The inserted image at the bottom showed the statistical distribution regarding the particle size of B-CDs, which was mainly concentrated in the range of 1.64 to 4.07 nm with average particle size of 2.53 nm. From the HR-TEM images in Fig. 1B, it could be seen that the synthesized B-CDs exhibited obvious lattice stripes, indicating their high crystallinity. The results confirmed the spherical feature with uniform size, excellent water solubility, and good dispersibility without self-aggregation in water of B-CDs. Their high water-solubility was mainly due to the hydrophilic carboxyl and amino groups contained in the precursor molecules of citric acid and urea, which were polymerized at high temperature and pressure to form a graphene-like CN conjugated carbon core structure. Then, some hydrophilic groups, such as carboxyl, amino, and amide groups from the precursor molecules were included on the surface of B-CDs.

Then, the optical properties of the synthesized B-CDs were investigated. The excitation-emission three-dimensional (3D) fluorescence map in Fig. 1C exhibited a single emission center with an excitation (EX) wavelength at 350 nm and an emission (EM) wavelength at 435 nm in the emission wavelength range of 360–500 nm, indicating the independent blue emission of the B-CDs under UV excitation at 350 nm, which was helpful for establishing the blue fluorescence signal-based sensor. Then, the UV–visible absorption (Abs) spectrum, as well as fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the B-CDs were recorded in Fig. 1D. It could be found from the UV–visible spectrum (black curve) that the absorption wavelength of the B-CDs was mainly in the UV region of 200–400 nm, and the first UV absorption peak near 230 nm might originate from the π-π* transition of the C=C/C=N conjugated system in the carbon core, while, the second UV absorption peak near 340 nm might be dominated by the n-π * transitions of the functional groups on the surface of B-CDs (Jia et al., 2023). The fluorescence excitation spectrum (red curve) of the B-CDs at an EM wavelength of 435 nm in Fig. 1D indicated a maximum EX wavelength around 350 nm, which almost overlapped with the second UV absorption peak at 340 nm of them. Thus, it could be inferred that the blue fluorescence emission of the B-CDs mainly came from the ultraviolet absorption generated by the internal n-π * transitions. The inset image of the B-CDs solution in Fig. 1D showed a colorless clear solution of the B-CDs under sunlight, and bright blue fluorescence under a 365-nm UV excitation, which was verified by the optimal fluorescence emission (blue curve) at 435 nm. In addition, from the fluorescence emission spectra of B-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (320–400 nm) in Fig. 1E, it could be seen that the emission peaks remained near 435 nm without any shifting, indicating the excellent non-excitation dependent fluorescence characteristics of the B-CDs.

AuNCs with bright red fluorescence emission were prepared through the reaction between BSA as a reductant and chloroauric acid, the morphology and optical properties of which were characterized by using the TEM, HR-TEM, UV, and fluorescence strategies.

The TEM image in Fig. 2A displayed the uniform spherical structures and good dispersion without aggregation of R-AuNCs aqueous solution. The inset image at the top displayed the high crystallinity of R-AuNCs with an interplanar distance of 0.23 nm, which was consistent with the crystal plane spacing of Au. Through statistical analysis of 106 R-AuNCs in the inset histogram at the bottom, it was concluded that their particle sizes ranged from 1.73 to 3.81 nm with an average particle size of 2.70 nm. Clear lattice stripes could be observed from the HR-TEM image in Fig. 2B. These findings confirmed the successful obtainment of R-AuNCs with spherical feature in uniform size, excellent dispersibility and water solubility.

Furthermore, the optical features of the prepared R-AuNCs were studied. From the excitation-emission 3D fluorescence image in Fig. 2C, an independent fluorescence emission center located at EX wavelength of 353 nm and EM wavelength of 644 nm in the red fluorescence region was observed for the R-AuNCs. And the emission center at 644 nm provided good possibility for constructing a red fluorescence signal-based sensor. Fig. 2D showed the UV absorption spectrum (black curve), emission spectra (blue curve) and fluorescence excitation (red curve) of R-AuNCs. It could be found that the light absorption region of the R-AuNCs was mainly located in the region of 200–300 nm, and red fluorescence emission could be realized by using a UV flashlight at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm, providing convenience for the subsequent smartphone-based color recognition and detection. The inset image showed the light brown-yellow solution of R-AuNCs under sunlight, and bright red fluorescence under a 365-nm UV excitation. The large stokes shift of approximately 300 nm between the fluorescence excitation (red) and emission (blue) curves also confirmed that the prepared R-AuNCs could obtain bright red fluorescence under a 365-nm UV excitation with the optimal fluorescence emission peak located around 644 nm. Moreover, the fluorescence emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths (300–500 nm) in Fig. 2E indicated the emission peaks at 435 nm without any fluctuation, verifying the good non-excitation dependent fluorescence feature of R-AuNCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility in constructing the ratiometric fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra of R-AuNCs and B-CDs with the addition of different concentrations of GLY solutions were recorded in Fig. 3 to explore the effect of the added GLY on their optical performance and to access the feasibility in constructing the ratiometric fluorescent sensing strategy.

It could be found that the introduction of GLY solution caused remarkable change in the fluorescence intensity of both B-CDs and R-AuNCs. As shown in Fig. 3A, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs at 435 nm gradually was lowered with increasing the added amount of GLY in the range of 0–9 μg/mL, while that for R-AuNCs at 640 nm (Fig. 3B) was enhanced with increasing the concentration of GLY from 0 to 10 μg/mL. The GLY-caused diametrically opposite changes in the fluorescence intensity of the two fluorescent probes indicated that both B-CDs and R-AuNCs could serve as ideal signal indicators in fabricating a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for glyphosate detection with higher sensitivity and accuracy.

Then, the ratiometric fluorescent probes with blue and red dual-signal emission were obtained by mixing B-CDs and R-AuNCs solutions in a certain proportion. Fortunately, after combination, the optimal excitation wavelength of both B-CDs and R-AuNCs was located near 350 nm in the ultraviolet region, meaning that the blue and red fluorescence dual-signal emissions could be achieved under a 350-nm excitation. More importantly, both fluorescent probes exhibited outstanding non-excitation dependence, and the change in excitation wavelength did not affect the emission peak position of them. Therefore, 350 nm was chosen as the optimal excitation wavelength for the ratiometric fluorescent probes. As shown in Fig. 4A, under a 350-nm UV excitation, the ratiometric fluorescent probe exhibited obvious blue and red emission peaks at 435 nm and 635 nm, respectively. Due to the possible interaction between B-CDs and R-AuNCs, the fluorescence emission intensity of them after mixing slightly decreased, but which did not affect the fluorescence performance of B-CDs/R-AuNCs ratiometric probes as the signal indicators for the quantitation of GLY. By calculating the fluorescence intensity ratio value of the two emission peaks, sensitive and accurate detection of GLY could be achieved. To further explore the morphology of B-CDs and R-AuNCs after mixing, the TEM image of B-CDs/R-AuNCs ratiometric probes was recorded. As shown in Fig. 4B, both B-CDs and R-AuNCs still presented good dispersity in the mixed aqueous solution with their respective regular spherical shapes, which might be due to the electrostatic repulsion between them. The zeta potentials of −11.2 mV, −2.9 mV, and − 15.2 mV for B-CDs, R-AuNCs, and B-CDs/R-AuNCs ratiometric probes, respectively, in Fig. 4C provided evidences regarding the electrostatic repulsion effect between B-CDs and R-AuNCs.

Subsequently, the stability of the B-CDs/R-AuNCs ratiometric probes in salt solution and storage for a long time was investigated through calculating the fluorescence intensity ratio value (I 435/I 635) at 435 nm to 635 nm. Fig. 4D indicated that the I 435/I 635 values remained stable with increasing the concentration of NaCl from 0 to 100 mM, confirming the good stability of B-CDs/R-AuNCs probes towards salt ions. Then, the freshly-prepared B-CDs/R-AuNCs probe aqueous solution was stored at 4 °C away from light to measure the fluorescence spectrum in a 24-h interval. Fig. 4E displayed stable I 435/I 635 values within 7 days, illustrating the excellent storage stability of the mixed probes. All these were beneficial for constructing a stable and reliable B-CDs/R-AuNCs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor.

To further evaluate the feasibility of the proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensing strategy for GLY detection, the fluorescence spectra of the B-CDs/R-AuNCs probes in the presence of different concentrations of GLY were measured in Fig. 4F. It could be found that the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs at 435 nm remarkably decreased, while, that of R-AuNCs at 635 nm gradually increased with increasing the added concentration of GLY from 0.3 to 7 μg/mL. These results verified the successful establishment of ratiometric fluorescent sensor for GLY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism regarding GLY-induced fluorescence changes of B-CDs and R-AuNCs
Fluorescence quenching mechanism of GLY on B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of some materials on CDs refer to the FRET (Förster resonance energy transfer), IFE (inner filter effect), DQE (dynamic quenching effect), and SQE (static quenching effect) (Li et al., 2021). Here, the GLY-induced fluorescence quenching mechanism on B-CDs was discussed through recording the UV absorption and fluorescence spectra, as well as determining the fluorescence lifetime.

The UV absorption spectrum in Fig. 5A showed a light absorption band of GLY at 200–250 nm, which did not overlap with the fluorescence excitation spectrum of B-CDs at 350 nm and the emission spectrum of R-AuNCs at 435 nm. Therefore, it could be concluded that GLY-induced fluorescence quenching on B-CDs was not due to FRET (Förster resonance energy transfer) and IFE (inner filter effect) mechanisms. Then, the effect of GLY on the UV absorption spectrum of B-CDs was analyzed. As shown in Fig. 5B, the introduction of GLY only caused the reduction of the UV absorption intensity of B-CDs carbon dots, but no change on the peak position, indicating that no new non-fluorescent complexes were formed on the surface of B-CDs during the quenching process. Thus, SQE (static quenching effect) did not occurred. On the other hand, in static quenching mode, the fluorescence lifetime will remain unchanged. Therefore, the effect of GLY on the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs was analyzed. The fluorescence lifetime decay curves in Fig. 5C showed that the addition of GLY reduced the fluorescence lifetime of B-CDs from 7.39 ns to 6.52 ns, which might be attributed to the dynamic quenching of ionic bonds between amino, carboxyl, and phosphate groups (Clermont-Paquette et al., 2023). Hence, it was inferred that GLY as a quenching agent significantly reduced the fluorescence lifetime and quenched the fluorescence of B-CDs through DQE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence enhancement mechanism of GLY on R-AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: It was reported that the fluorescence response improvement of AuNCs was mainly attributed to the aggregation-induced enhancement (AIE) (Zhang et al., 2023). To verify this speculation, the particle size and morphology of R-AuNCs before and after the introduction of GLY were observed through recording the TEM images.

As shown in Fig. S1(A), the R-AuNCs nanoparticles exhibited good dispersity in aqueous solution without any aggregation. While, obvious deposition with larger size of nanoparticles than single R-AuNCs was clearly observed in Fig. S1(B) after introducing GLY. Also, these aggregates presented significantly uneven distribution. Therefore, it could be concluded that the increased fluorescence intensity of R-AuNCs caused by GLY was mainly due to the AIE mechanism.

In order to achieve high-performance sensing of GLY, some crucial parameters, such as the mixed proportion of B-CDs to R-AuNCs, solution pH, type of buffer solution, and incubation time, in constructing the ratiometric fluorescent sensor, were firstly optimized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mixed proportion of B-CDs to R-AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: To make the constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensing strategy more suitable for the subsequent smartphone-based detection, an obvious fluorescence color change from blue to red caused by GLY should be realized by adjusting the added volume proportion of B-CDs to R-AuNCs. Here, the fluorescence color changes of four ratiometric probe solutions containing B-CDs to R-AuNCs at 5:1, 5:2, 5:3, 5:4 (v/v) induced by different concentrations (0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 μg/mL) of GLY were compared.

Fig. 6 showed that, with increasing the added volume of R-AuNCs in the probe solution, the emission peak intensity of R-AuNCs at 635 nm was enhanced, in accompany with significant fluorescence color change. As presented in Fig. 6A, when the volume ratio of B-CDs to R-AuNCs was set as 5:1 (v/v) and with increasing the added amount of GLY from 0 to 10 μg/mL, the ratiometric probe solution exhibited obvious fluorescence color changes from dark blue, baby blue, light red to deep red that could be easily recognized by the naked eye or a smartphone. When the added proportion of R-AuNCs in probe solution from 5:2, 5:3 to 5:4 (Fig. 6B, C, D), the fluorescence color changes were not conspicuous with pink, brownish red and red fluorescence as the predominant signal outputs. Thus, to facilitate the subsequent spectrometer-based and smartphone-assisted recognition and detection, the mixed proportion of B-CDs to R-AuNCs was fixed at 5:1 to prepare the ratiometric probe solution.

The sensing system pH will influence the protonation and deprotonation efficacy of fluorophores, as well as the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs and R-AuNCs in the ratiometric probes to cause the changes in I 435/I 635 value. Then, the most suitable solution environment for GLY detection could be achieved by adding an acid or alkali solution to adjust the pH of the sensing system. As shown in Fig. S2(A), when the sensing system was controlled at pH 4, 5, 6, 7, 8 and 9, the maximum I 435/I 635 value was obtained at pH 7, which might be ascribed to the increased fluorescence intensity of B-CDs at 435 nm in their protonation process. When the detection system was in a neutral even to alkaline environment, the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs at 435 nm reached the strongest and most stabile value. The protonation process of R-AuNCs in an alkaline sensing environment at pH 8 and 9 also slightly enhanced the fluorescence intensity of the ratiometric probe at 635 nm, resulting in a lower I 435/I 635 value than that was obtained in the neutral environment at pH 7. In addition, due to the relatively low proportion of R-AuNCs in the ratiometric probe, the impact of pH change in the detection system on the fluorescence intensity of R-AuNCs was much smaller than that of B-CDs. Thus, the pH of the sensing system was adjusted to a neutral environment at pH 7 to obtain the highest initial I 435/I 635 value.

The selection of buffer solution is important to keep a stable pH environment, thereby reducing the influence of the changed pH on the sensing system. The diverse conjugated acid-base pairs contained in different buffer solutions exhibit varying effects on the sensing process even under the same pH conditions. To find a buffer solution system that was more suitable for the proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensing strategy, five different solution systems including 0.01 M HEPES, MES, PBS, TAE, and ultrapure water (UW) at pH 7 were investigated through recording the ΔI 435/I 635 value caused by 2 μg/mL of GLY. As shown in Fig. S2(B), the ΔI 435/I 635 values were low when HEPES, MES, PBS, and TAE were used, which might be due to the adverse effects of different ions in these four buffer solutions on the sensing process of GLY. While, the highest ΔI 435/I 635 value was got when UW was chosen as the buffer solution, which displayed the strongest sensitivity to GLY.

To ensure that the ratiometric fluorescence probes could rapidly, fully and completely recognize GLY molecule, the incubation time of GLY with the B-CDs/R-AuNCs fluorescence probe system was studied within 30 min. As seen in Fig. S2(C), the I 435/I 635 value decreased with increasing the incubation time from 0 to 30 min, and reached a turning point at 4 min, and then gradually remained stable after 6 min. Therefore, 6 min were regarded as the optimal incubation time for GLY with the B-CDs/R-AuNCs probes in the ratiometric fluorescent sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the B-CDs/R-AuNCs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Based on GLY-induced fluorescence quenching on B-CDs and enhancement on R-AuNCs, the developed B-CDs/R-AuNCs dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensor was applied for the quantitative analysis of this pesticide under the optimized conditions through mixing the ratiometric probe solution with different concentrations (0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10, and 12 μg/mL) of GLY solution to record the fluorescence spectra. Then, the fluorescence intensities of B-CDs at 435 nm and R-AuNCs at 635 nm in the presence of GLY were monitored to calculate the I 435/I 635 values (y) and further to fit the relationship between the fluorescence emission peak intensity ratio (I 435/I 635) of the fluorescence spectrum and the concentration (x) of GLY. As shown in Fig. 7A, a good linear relationship between I 435/I 635 and the added concentration of GLY was observed in the concentration range of 0.3–12 μg/mL with linear equation y = −0.95× + 12.16 (R
2 = 0.997). Subsequently, through measuring the I 435/I 635 values of 10 blank solutions without the addition of GLY and based on the formula LOD = (3σ/k) (where σ was the relative standard deviation of the blank solution, and k was the slope), the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.218 μg/mL. The above results indicated that the proposed ratiometric fluorescent sensor to GLY ratio fluorescent nano-sensor could not only rapidly detect GLY at extremely low residual level, but also quantify it in a wide concentration range. Importantly, compared with other type of recently reported sensing methods for GLY detection in Table 1 , the newly-developed B-CDs/R-AuNCs dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensor in this work exhibited excellent sensitivity and better performance, presenting broad applications to achieve rapid and sensitive quantitative detection of GLY within 6 min with the help of a fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective evaluation of the dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the anti-interference ability of the B-CDs/R-AuNCs dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensor, the selectivity was examined through comparing the influence of GLY and other five commonly-found pesticides, such as carbendazim, malathion, acetamiprid, dimethoate, and chlorpyrifos, on the I 435/I 635 values. As presented in Fig. 7B, under the same experimental conditions, the addition of high concentrations (16 μg/mL) of other five interfering pesticides could not cause significant changes in I 435/I 635 values, which were all larger than 10, indicating that B-CDs and R-AuNCs did not exhibit significant fluorescence response to them, as well as their low or negligible influence on the ratiometric fluorescent probes. While, 8 μg/mL of GLY that was only 1/2 of the concentration of the five interfering pesticide molecules could cause a remarkable decrease in the I 435/I 635 value (less than 5), illustrating its significant effect on the fluorescence of the dual-signal probes. This verified the excellent selectivity of the ratiometric fluorescent sensor for GLY molecule, which might be mainly due to the good sensitivity and specificity of the synthesized B-CDs and R-AuNCs fluorescent materials to GLY. Thus, the newly-developed dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensor could achieve highly selective and specific determination of GLY in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor for high-throughput detection of GLY
ce:section.ce:para: The constructed ratiometric fluorescent sensor in this study exhibited excellent visual detection potential by the naked eye with the color of B-CDs/R-AuNCs probes changing from blue to red as increasing the added concentration of GLY. However, the visual recognition still contained the subjectivity and randomness. Then, a smartphone that could automatically record the fluorescence color changes was introduced for intelligent analysis. Herein, the smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor was fabricated through adding B-CDs/R-AuNCs dual-signal probes and GLY in each hole of a 96-well plate (Scheme 1B). As shown in Fig. 8A, with increasing the introduced concentration of GLY from 0 to 11 μg/mL, the images in the 96-well plate under a 365-nm UV lamp excitation observed by the naked eye showed an obvious change from dark blue, baby blue, bluish violet, light red to deep red. The fluorescence color changes were then recorded and analyzed by using a smartphone to calculate the R/B ratio value (y) of the red channel to the blue channel, which exhibited a good linear relationship with the concentration (x) of glyphosate with an equation y = 0.17× + 0.24 (R 2 = 0.996) within a concentration range of 0–11 μg/mL regarding GLY. These results indicated that the smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensor could overcome the dependence on bulky and expensive spectrophotometers and could be realized in a 96-well plate for the rapid high-throughput analysis of multitudinous samples in one run.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GLY analysis in actual malt samples
ce:section.ce:para: CBZ residue was commonly measure in diverse food, plant, and herb matrices including medicinal malts. Thus, in this study, malt with great edible property and medicinal efficacy, as well as a large production and consumption was regarded as the actual sample to examine the reliability and practicality of the newly-developed B-CDs/R-AuNCs dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform for CBZ detection through recovery experiments by the standard addition method. The confirmed GLY-free malts were crushed into fine powder with the addition of low, medium and high concentrations (1, 5, and 10 μg/mL) of GLY standard for sample preparation according to the above procedure. The amount of GLY in the spiked sample was monitored by using the spectrophotometer-based and smartphone-assisted ratiometric fluorescent sensors, respectively, to calculate the recovery.

As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries measured by using a fluorescence spectrophotometer were in the range of 94.7–103.1% with an average value of 97.8%. The RSDs of three parallel experiments were all less than 3.5%, indicating the good accuracy and reliability of the spectrophotometer-based dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in-lab detection of GLY in real malt samples. Table 2 also illustrated that the recoveries by using a smartphone ranged from 94.1% to 107.0% with an average value of 97.8% and RSD lower than 7.0%. This indicated the good practicability of the smartphone-assisted fluorescent sensing platform for intelligent measurement of GLY in actual samples. The mutually-verified results regarding the recovery confirmed the great potential of smartphone for portable measurement of GLY in a large quantity of complex samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted paper-based sensor for GLY measurement
ce:section.ce:para: To further lowering the analytical cost and improve the on-site detection capability of the dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent sensing platform, a simple and intelligent smartphone-assisted paper-based sensor containing the B-CDs/R-AuNCs ratiometric fluorescence probes was established on the paper strip with direct deposition of different concentrations of GLY solutions. And the fluorescence color from each paper strip under excitation at 365 nm by using a UV lamp was recorded and automatically analyzed for the quantitation of GLY.

As shown in Fig. 8B, without GLY (0 μg/mL), the paper strips containing dual-signal B-CDs/R-AuNCs ratiometric probes exhibited bright blue color, and with the addition of GLY from 2 to 8 μg/mL, the blue fluorescence was darkened to baby blue, light pink, and dark red, indicating the significant GLY-induced fluorescence quenching on B-CDs and enhancement effect in this sensing system. These color changes on the were recorded by a smartphone and analyzed by the installed ‘Color Grab’ App. A satisfactory linear relationship was realized between the R/B ratio (y) of the R channel to B channel and GLY concentration (x) with equation y = 0.09× + 0.35 (R 2 = 0.995) and LOD of 0.527 μg/mL. These results illustrated that the proposed smartphone-assisted paper-based ratiometric fluorescent sensor was feasible for rapid on-site measurement of GLY in actual samples. Future work would concentrate on enhancing the reusability and practicability to broaden its real-world applications in more foods, environmental and clinical samples.

In summary, a novel “two swords combination” strategy based on smartphone-assisted B-CDs/R-AuNCs dual-signal ratiometric fluorescent and paper sensors was firstly designed for the in-lab sensitive detection and on-site rapid analysis of GLY. It integrated the superiorities of strong fluorescence emission of B-CDs and R-AuNCs, highly-improved detection accuracy of ratiometric fluorescence probes, excellent anti-interfering ability of dual-signal output mode, outstanding portability and accessibility of smartphone-based intelligent detection, which was well confirmed for GLY analysis in malt samples. This study provides new insights into developing the dual-signal ratiometric sensing platforms for simple, sensitive, cost-effective, and portable detection of more trace contaminants in foods, clinical and environmental samples. The exploration of more fluorescence materials to construct high-performance ratiometric probes for developing more and more reliable intelligent sensing platforms is undoubtedly a future research trend, which should be enhanced.

Tingting Di: Methodology, Investigation. Runran Ma: Formal analysis, Data curation. Doudou Lei: Validation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yining Zhang: Methodology, Investigation, Data curation.
